articles.title: Dowsing Experiments with a Medium - 1929
articles.excerpt: Dowsing experiments with Abbot Lambert and Greek mediums to detect underground water in Kolokynthou, Athens.

content.value: Trying to find water with the art of dowsing. Yesterday's experiments in Kolokynthou.
Abbot Lambert and his miraculous divining rod.

Yesterday at 9:30 a.m., the famous dowser Abbot Lambert, accompanied by the president of the Society for Psychical Research, Mr. Tanagras, the secretary-general and neurologist Mr. Vlastos, and the Greek mediums Miss Elpiniki Psaromilinga and Miss Tassia Stefanou, visited Kolokynthou to conduct dowsing experiments.

The system works as follows. The dowser — that is, the person gifted with the exceptional ability to sense water beneath the ground underfoot — walks while holding a flexible rod. When he passes over a vein of underground water, the rod bends downward on its own, drawn by some invisible force.

Abbot Lambert walked through various areas and verified the presence of underground water. The local residents, having heard the news, gathered to watch the experiments. At one of the houses in the area, a man showed the abbot a spot in his garden where a well had been dug years ago and had since dried up. Abbot Lambert passed over the spot and confirmed that there was still water below.

Abbot Lambert also tested the mediums who accompanied him, with modest but satisfactory results, given that this was the first time such an experiment of this kind had been conducted. Systematic training is required.

The following explanation is offered for the phenomenon. Water acts radioactively upon the medium's nervous system, concentrating in the metal strip held by the medium, which may even be an ordinary piece of wire.

image.alt: Our Writers as Mediums – Psychic Phenomena (1928)

articles.title: Our Writers as Mediums – A School Telepathy Experiment, 1928
articles.excerpt: The Society for Psychical Research documents telepathy, autosuggestion and premonition cases among prominent Greek writers, and presents a telepathy experiment at the Varvakeion Gymnasium with Professor G. Katsamas as medium.

content.value: The Society for Psychical Research has recently received reports of highly curious psychic phenomena of telepathy, autosuggestion, thought transference and the like, occurring among Greek men of letters.

It appears that our writers possess strange psychic properties — exceptional abilities to produce phenomena that resemble the supernatural. The Society for Psychical Research is now studying these cases scientifically.
We shall recount the phenomena and then add certain scientific observations — thoughts that demonstrate that our writers are first-rate mediums.
We begin with a phenomenon that recently occurred to the well-known and distinguished playwright, columnist and poet Mr. Timos Moraitinis.
One night he was suffering from a terrible toothache, which had reached such a pitch that it made him cry out in despair:
— Good heavens! Is there no saint to help me!

At that very moment, Mr. Moraitinis suddenly believed he saw before him the distinctive figure of a bearded saint, with the vivid impression that it was Saint Athanasios. At the same instant, the terrible pain ceased entirely.

Astonished by this event, and unable to trust his own senses, Mr. Moraitinis left his room and lit a cigarette. He felt no pain at all! He then ran and woke his mother to ask whether she had heard him crying out — and indeed she had.
The next morning Mr. Moraitinis awoke completely well and resolved to light a candle to the saint who had saved him from the toothache. He approached a coachman and asked:
— Are you an old Athenian? Do you know Athens well?
— Yes, the man replied.
— Then tell me, is there a church of Saint Athanasios somewhere? I cannot recall one, said Mr. Moraitinis.
The coachman told him he knew of a church of Saint Athanasios near the Theseion. Mr. Moraitinis got into the carriage and went there.

Once inside, he saw the icon of the saint. He was seized with amazement and felt a shiver run through him. In the icon of Saint Athanasios he recognised, unmistakably, the very figure he had seen at the moment his pain had ceased.

The following evening, walking on foot with a friend near midnight on the way home, he was recounting the astonishing event. At the exact moment they were crossing the underground railway bridge, he was saying:
— The moment I entered the church and looked upon the icon of Saint Athanasios, I recognised the figure I had seen at the moment of my invocation…
At that very moment, as Mr. Moraitinis was saying these words, he suddenly heard a voice that seemed to come from behind the bridge. The voice said:
— Yes! Yes! Yes!
Astonished, he ran towards the bridge, but no one else was there. He asked his friend whether he too had heard the voice. The friend, however, had heard nothing.
Another curious psychic phenomenon occurred to Mr. Dionysios Devaris, playwright and editor-in-chief of the Vradhyni newspaper.

One evening Mr. Devaris dreamed of great turmoil, commotion and priests at the home of his friend Mr. Petros Filippas, a former member of parliament, in Lefkada. In the morning he told the story to a colleague at the Vradhyni, who wrote it down. Three days later news arrived that Mr. Filippas had indeed died.
Another noteworthy phenomenon is recounted by the well-known short-story writer Mrs. Arsinoe Papadopoulou.

Her brother, Georgios Papadopoulos, had been studying at a military academy in Belgium and was returning to Athens upon completing his studies. Mrs. Papadopoulou had gone the previous evening to stay at her sister's house in Patisia.

As it was raining, Mrs. Papadopoulou was obliged to remain and sleep there that night. She then dreamed of her mother, who had died five years earlier, seated before a marble table and eating something.
— What are you eating, mother? Mrs. Papadopoulou asked her in the dream.
— I have taken out my heart and am eating it, was the reply.

Mrs. Papadopoulou then woke. The night was stormy, and she reflected that her brother was at that very hour crossing the Adriatic and might be in danger. She looked at the clock: the time was one in the morning.
The following evening her brother arrived ill and feverish. When they told him that Arsinoe had seen him in danger that night, he said:
— It is true. I was in danger.
And he recounted that during the voyage, while crossing the Adriatic, he had been seized by a violent cough and had suffered a haemorrhage. Two months later he died of galloping consumption.
We have here, then, phenomena that occurred to our writers — phenomena explained scientifically by suggestion, autosuggestion, telepathy and thought transference.

In the case of Mr. Moraitinis, the final scene on the bridge is scientifically explained as a hallucination arising from the state of intense psychic excitement in which he found himself while recounting the strange event. Hallucinations of this kind have been documented in the literature of the Society for Psychical Research.

This hallucinatory phenomenon is a sign that Mr. Moraitinis is, as it is termed, an 'intensely radioactive individual' — that is, a medium. The first event, the healing of the toothache, is also explained as a powerful instance of autosuggestion, which in a medium can produce remarkable cures.
And the dream of Mr. Devaris, and the dream and premonition of Mrs. Arsinoe Papadopoulou, are phenomena of powerful telepathy — observed only in mediums.

The Society for Psychical Research, after extensive studies and on the basis of such phenomena, has formed the opinion that 'all artists of today are recognised as intensely radioactive individuals (mediums) of the first order'. The SPR is now investigating why this should be so.
Related to this property is the phenomenon of stage fright that afflicts performers — an involuntary faltering, a nervous absence that seems inexplicable.
Must we then accept that all our artists and writers are mediums?

But many others among our intellectuals are also mediums. A recent experiment proved that even one of our finest mathematicians, Professor G. Katsamas of the Varvakeion Gymnasium, is a medium of the highest order.
But let us describe the experiment.
1. The shape drawn on the blackboard of Class B, transmitted mentally by the pupils. The shape received mentally by Professor G. Katsamas. (illustration)

value.alt: Shape drawn on the school blackboard during the telepathy experiment

content.value: At the Varvakeion Gymnasium, telepathy experiments were conducted between pupils of two classes. Class B acted as transmitter under the guidance of teacher Mr. Chrysanthakopoulou, and Class D as receiver.

Specifically, a pupil drew a shape on the blackboard of Class B — a square with its diagonals — and the entire class stared at it attentively for five minutes, while simultaneously Class D concentrated its attention on receiving the mental impression.
This was the experiment. It appears to have succeeded, as most pupils drew a square and others drew the interior triangles of the shape.
Among the receivers in Class D was Professor G. Katsamas. He received a square as well, but inside it he also perceived the figure of a boy.
What had happened? In the transmitting class, one pupil had drawn the shape on the blackboard while the others watched.
It seems, therefore, that from the pupils' thoughts the image of the boy who was drawing had passed into the mind of the medium, Professor Katsamas, who described his impression as follows:

— On the opposite wall I saw a small shadowy square, like those in itinerant photographers' pictures, and within it, at first indistinct and then clearer, the image of a young boy of about eleven, with a broad forehead and dark hair, holding something in his hand — rather like a cup.
After saying this, Professor Katsamas entered Class B and immediately identified the pupil Filippos Boukas, who was precisely the one who had drawn the shape.
It was then explained that the 'cup-like object' seen by Professor Katsamas was the piece of chalk that the pupil had been holding while drawing the shape.
Thus not only three of our most celebrated writers, but also a mathematician, were proved to be mediums with remarkable psychic properties.
Source: newspaper EVDHOMAS, 01.09.1928, pp. 3–4.

articles.author: Ακρόπολις

image.alt: Psychometric and telepathy experiments at the Society for Psychical Research

articles.title: Experiments – Staged Crimes | Mediums and Police Training 1934
articles.excerpt: Psychometry, telepathy and post-hypnotic suggestion experiments were conducted at the Society for Psychical Research before senior and junior police officers.

content.value: Last evening, interesting psychometric experiments were conducted at the Society for Psychical Research before a group of police officers.
These experiments were organised by the president of the Society for Psychical Research, Mr. Tanagras, especially for the benefit of the Police.

Those in attendance included police directors Mr. Petounis and Mr. Koresis, police officers Mr. Zagklis, Mr. Gialamas, Mr. Tsankaris, Mr. Pantelis and Mr. Alexopoulos, as well as deputy officers Mr. Archimandrítis, Mr. Karampetsos, Mr. Panetsos, Mr. Kapitsoğlu and others.
The Society's mediums took part in the experiments, among them Mrs. Konstantia Nikolaidou, Kassandra, Kleio Georgiou, Eleni Zakynthinoy, Androniki Chalkiadaki, Eleni Kikidou and Elpida Pavlatou.

At the opening of the session, Mr. Tanagras explained the significance of the experiments and the role that psychometric methods would play in the future — particularly post-hypnotic interrogation of criminals.
First, psychometric experiments were performed, during which the mediums succeeded in identifying, from among identical cards, those corresponding to the attending police officers.
This was followed by telekinesis experiments by the medium Kleio Georgiou, who caused the remote deflection and stoppage of a compass needle.
Subsequently, the police officers, under the guidance of Mr. Tanagras, performed telepathy experiments with the Society's mediums, with several achieving notable results.
Deputy officer Mr. Panetsos attempted a telepathy experiment with Androniki Chalkiadaki in connection with a real missing-persons case, bringing to light unknown details of the investigation.
The most compelling part of the session was the staging of crimes, in which mediums under post-hypnotic suggestion enacted the role of the criminal.
In the first experiment, the medium Konstantia carried out the hypnotist's instructions with precision, enacting a staged murder with a dummy knife.
In the second experiment, Mr. Kapitsoğlu enacted a staged assault and, after his fictitious arrest, falsely denounced another person as the perpetrator.
The third experiment was performed by Mrs. Chalkiadaki, who, due to an intense emotional reaction, was unable to complete her instructions and broke down in tears.
